Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered throughout the course. I'd usually dedicate about 10 hours a week, but those hours weren't always at the same time. I preferred reading and writing things out, so I'd often take notes from the materials and then study from those instead of going back to the LMS repeatedly. I tried to find pockets of time between other responsibilities, which meant my routine shifted week to week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I'd have weeks where I barely touched the course materials, and then other weeks where I'd cram everything in before a deadline. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work. I think having medium resources meant I had to balance a lot of different priorities, so consistency was hard to maintain.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment notification would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd check in just to see if there were any announcements or if I'd missed something important. The gamified tasks actually caught my attention a few times because they felt less intimidating than regular assignments. But honestly, if nothing was pressing, I might go days without logging in.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, especially when the gamified elements were introduced. I liked earning points or seeing progress, it gave me a small sense of accomplishment. But that routine never fully stuck because my attendance and engagement were already pretty inconsistent. It felt more like occasional habit than a true daily practice.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I could download them, print them out, and annotate them at my own pace. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful for seeing other perspectives, but I relied heavily on text-based content. Videos were fine but I often found myself skimming through them rather than watching fully.
Q6: I often ignored the longer video lectures or only watched portions of them. They just didn't match my learning style as well as reading did. I also tended to delay discussion posts until I absolutely had to participate. Some of the optional supplementary materials I skipped entirely because I was already struggling to keep up with the required content.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what I needed to know for assignments or exams. If something helped me understand a concept I was struggling with or contributed to my grade, I was more motivated. The gamified tasks felt worthwhile because they gave immediate feedback and made me feel like I was making progress even on small things.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When I was already behind on required work, optional stuff felt like extra weight rather than an opportunity. Required activities had urgency attached to them, so I prioritized those. If I had more time and fewer competing demands, I might have explored optional content more.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them on my own without being connected to the LMS. I also kept a notebook where I wrote out summaries and key concepts. Sometimes I'd look up additional explanations online if the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials. I also did practice problems on paper that I never submitted anywhere. My understanding was growing, but none of that effort registered in the system.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it, and then never open it in the LMS again. All my subsequent studying with that material happened offline. It was just more convenient to have everything saved locally where I could access it anytime without needing internet or logging in.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement became harder, usually when multiple deadlines piled up or when I felt overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind. My attendance was really low, and catching up felt daunting sometimes. During those periods, I'd just focus on survival mode—doing the minimum to pass rather than truly engaging.
Q13: Competing responsibilities affected my LMS use quite a bit. With medium resources, I had to juggle different things in my life, and sometimes the course took a backseat. Fatigue also played a role; after a long day, the last thing I wanted to do was log in and watch videos. I'd often postpone studying until I felt more mentally ready.
Q14: There were times I'd log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd have the page open but my mind was elsewhere, thinking about other things I needed to handle. It felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning. Those sessions didn't contribute much to my understanding.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. Sometimes the task felt too big or I wasn't confident I understood the material well enough. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I was more prepared, but then later would become last minute. It's a pattern I'm aware of but struggle to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. Without a deadline looming, I'd often neglect the course. But when something was due, I'd suddenly become very active—logging in multiple times, reviewing materials, submitting work. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but it's how I managed to pass despite my low attendance.
Q17: Feedback did affect my engagement, especially when it was encouraging. Getting good scores on assignments motivated me to keep going. When feedback was delayed or vague, I felt less connected to the course. The gamified elements provided quick feedback which I appreciated—it kept me more engaged than waiting days for a grade.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace when life got busy. Bad because without strict structure, I'd let things slide. Having 13 online courses to manage meant I sometimes lost track of what was due when, so clearer structure might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, manageable pieces. That felt less overwhelming than big assignments. However, the sheer volume of content and the expectation to watch videos regularly was harder for me as a read-write learner. More text-based alternatives would have suited me better.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to figure out what's important or how the course works. They might also be struggling to understand the material, so they keep accessing resources without feeling confident enough to submit work. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that make it hard to follow through on assignments despite their intentions.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. My exam score and assignment scores were decent even though my LMS activity and attendance were low. I did a lot of work offline—reading, note-taking, reviewing on my own. The LMS didn't capture that effort, but it showed up in my performance when it mattered.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students are most active or which resources get accessed frequently. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just clicking through. They also miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the conversations with classmates outside the platform. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for a grade. It's when I'm curious about the content and want to learn more, when I'm actively thinking rather than passively consuming. For me, engagement also means finding ways to connect the material to things that matter to me personally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept going even when I fell behind or felt overwhelmed. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with my notebook, working through concepts on my own. It also wouldn't capture the mental effort of balancing this course with everything else in my life, or how the gamified elements actually gave me small moments of motivation when I needed them most.
